name: Homejoy
summary: On-demand home cleaning marketplace backed by Google Ventures shut down after burning through cash and facing worker-classification lawsuits.
lesson: Subsidized on-demand marketplaces can collapse quickly when customer acquisition costs outpace retention and legal risk mounts.

name: Katerra
summary: SoftBank-backed construction technology company tried to vertically integrate design, manufacturing, and building, but collapsed into bankruptcy.
lesson: Vertically integrating a capital-intensive industrial business too fast, funded by growth-stage capital, can outrun operational competence.

name: Managed by Q
summary: Office management and facilities-services marketplace acquired by WeWork; operations were wound down as WeWork retrenched during its own crisis.
lesson: An acquisition by a troubled parent company can doom an otherwise-functioning business.

name: Zenefits
summary: HR and insurance-brokerage software company grew explosively then was engulfed by a licensing-compliance scandal in 2016, leading to leadership ousters and a diminished eventual wind-down.
lesson: Growth-at-all-costs culture in a regulated industry can create existential compliance risk.

name: Fast Radius
summary: On-demand digital manufacturing company went public via SPAC then filed for bankruptcy after failing to control costs.
lesson: SPAC-funded manufacturing companies with heavy fixed costs need real revenue traction to survive as public companies.

name: Tradeshift
summary: B2B procurement and supply-chain platform once valued near $2.7B ran into severe financial distress, restructuring and selling off assets after years of heavy losses.
lesson: Raising enormous sums for a broad procurement platform vision without matching revenue can lead to a slow-motion collapse even at unicorn scale.

name: MedMen
summary: Cannabis retail chain that branded itself as the 'Apple Store of weed' expanded aggressively across multiple states, then saw its stock collapse over 99% and default on debt.
lesson: Aggressive multi-state retail expansion can collapse fast when unit economics never catch up to store count.

name: Rethink Robotics
summary: Maker of the Baxter and Sawyer collaborative industrial robots shut down after failing to find enough paying manufacturing customers.
lesson: Being technically groundbreaking in industrial robotics isn't sufficient if factory customers aren't yet ready to adopt at scale.

name: Bossa Nova Robotics
summary: Retail shelf-scanning robotics company whose flagship deployment with Walmart ended, after which the company ceased its retail-robotics operations.
lesson: Depending on a single anchor retail customer leaves a hardware startup dangerously exposed to one contract decision.

name: Desktop Metal
summary: Industrial metal 3D-printing manufacturer went public via SPAC, made a string of acquisitions, then saw its stock collapse and a planned merger with Stratasys collapse amid mounting losses.
lesson: Rolling up multiple acquisitions to build a manufacturing platform can multiply integration and cash-burn risk instead of synergies.

name: Casetext
summary: AI-powered legal research platform, creator of the CoCounsel AI legal assistant, acquired by Thomson Reuters in 2023 for approximately $650 million.
lesson: Building genuinely differentiated AI tooling for a well-defined professional workflow can produce a strong strategic exit.

name: Clio
summary: Cloud-based legal practice management software for small and mid-size law firms became one of legal tech's largest unicorns.
lesson: Focusing relentlessly on the operational needs of an underserved segment built durable category leadership.

name: Toptal
summary: Freelance marketplace connecting businesses with vetted top-tier software developers grew to hundreds of millions in revenue without venture funding.
lesson: A rigorous quality-screening process can be a durable competitive moat for a talent marketplace, even without outside capital.

name: Upwork
summary: Global freelance-talent marketplace formed from the merger of oDesk and Elance completed a successful IPO in 2018.
lesson: Consolidating fragmented freelance marketplaces via merger can create the scale needed for a durable public company.

name: ZipRecruiter
summary: Job-listing distribution and recruiting-marketplace platform completed a direct listing on the NYSE in 2021 as a profitable, cash-generative business.
lesson: Distributing job postings across many channels efficiently, then monetizing employer demand, can build a highly profitable marketplace.

name: Checkr
summary: Background-check and identity-verification technology company for employers and gig platforms reached a multibillion-dollar valuation.
lesson: Modernizing background-check infrastructure with an API-first approach let Checkr ride the growth of the gig economy without being a gig platform itself.

name: Thumbtack
summary: Home-services marketplace connecting consumers with local professionals across hundreds of categories reached a multibillion-dollar private valuation.
lesson: Broad category coverage combined with a lead-generation monetization model let Thumbtack outlast many narrower on-demand competitors.

name: Rover
summary: Pet-sitting and dog-walking marketplace completed an IPO in 2021 and was later acquired and taken private by Blackstone in 2024 at a premium.
lesson: A defensible two-sided marketplace with recurring demand can generate enough investor confidence for a premium buyout.

name: Xometry
summary: On-demand manufacturing marketplace connecting businesses needing custom parts with a network of manufacturers completed a successful IPO in 2021.
lesson: An asset-light marketplace model for manufacturing avoided the capital intensity that sank several vertically integrated manufacturing startups.

name: Samsara
summary: Industrial IoT platform providing sensor-based fleet and equipment monitoring completed a strong IPO in 2021.
lesson: Combining hardware sensors with a durable software/data subscription created recurring revenue that public investors rewarded.
